section.title: Jonah Flees and the Storm
section.sectionRationale: The book's opening movement: God's call to Nineveh, Jonah's flight in the opposite direction, and the storm that God hurls after him. It runs from the word of the LORD to the moment the pagan sailors, not the prophet, end up worshipping the true God.
section.thesis: A prophet runs from the God of the sea and the dry land, yet God pursues him through a storm, and the very sailors he endangers come to fear and worship the LORD.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: the call and the flight; the storm and the sleeping prophet; the casting overboard and the sailors' worship.

units.label: The call and the flight
units.note: down to Joppa, down into the ship

verses.text: Now the word of the LORD came to Jonah son of Amittai, saying,
“Get up! Go to the {{gadol|great}} city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before Me.”

Jonah, however, got up to flee to Tarshish, away from the presence of the LORD. He went down to Joppa and found a ship bound for Tarshish. So he paid the fare and went aboard to sail for Tarshish, away from the presence of the LORD.

units.label: The storm and the sleeping prophet

verses.text: Then the LORD {{hurl|hurled}} a great wind upon the sea, and such a violent storm arose that the ship was in danger of breaking apart.

The sailors were afraid, and each cried out to his own god. And they {{hurl|threw}} the ship’s cargo into the sea to lighten the load. But Jonah had gone down to the lowest part of the vessel, where he lay down and fell into a deep sleep.
The captain approached him and said, “How can you sleep? Get up and call upon your God. Perhaps this God will consider us, so that we may not perish.”
“Come!” said the sailors to one another. “Let us cast lots to find out who is responsible for this {{raah|calamity}} that is upon us.” So they cast lots, and the lot fell on Jonah.
“Tell us now,” they demanded, “who is to blame for this calamity that is upon us? What is your occupation, and where have you come from? What is your country, and who are your people?”
“I am a Hebrew,” replied Jonah. “I {{yare|worship}} the LORD, the God of the heavens, who made the sea and the dry land.”
Then the men were even more afraid and said to him, “What have you done?” The men knew that he was fleeing from the presence of the LORD, because he had told them.

units.label: Cast into the sea, and the sailors' worship
units.note: the storm quiets, the pagans fear the LORD

verses.text: Now the sea was growing worse and worse, so they said to Jonah, “What must we do to you to calm this sea for us?”
“Pick me up,” he answered, “and {{hurl|cast}} me into the sea, so it may quiet down for you. For I know that I am to blame for this violent storm that has come upon you.”
Nevertheless, the men rowed hard to get back to dry land, but they could not, for the sea was raging against them more and more.
So they cried out to the LORD: “Please, O LORD, do not let us perish on account of this man’s life! Do not charge us with innocent blood! For You, O LORD, have done as You pleased.”
Then they picked up Jonah and cast him into the sea, and the raging sea grew calm.
Then the men {{yare|feared}} the LORD greatly, and they offered a sacrifice to the LORD and made vows to Him.

gadol.gloss: Great, large. The word runs like a drumbeat through Jonah: the great city, the great wind, the great storm, the great fish, the sailors who fear greatly. It keeps the reader's eye on the sheer scale of what God is doing.

hurl.gloss: To hurl or throw with force. The same verb describes the LORD hurling the wind, the sailors hurling the cargo, and Jonah being hurled into the sea. God and the storm are the true actors; everyone else is thrown about by the wind He sends.

raah.gloss: Evil, disaster, or calamity. The same word names Nineveh's wickedness in verse 2 and the calamity the sailors dread here. It threads through the whole book, binding together human evil, the disaster it deserves, and God's response.

yare.gloss: To fear, revere, or worship. Jonah claims to fear the LORD even as he flees Him, while the pagan sailors move from being merely afraid to fearing the LORD greatly with sacrifice and vows. The irony is sharp: the outsiders worship, the prophet runs.

basic.context: God tells His prophet Jonah to go and preach to Nineveh, the capital of Assyria, Israel's most feared enemy. Instead Jonah runs the opposite way, boarding a ship for Tarshish. So God sends a great storm. The heart of the scene is surprising: the pagan sailors end up worshipping the true God while His own prophet sleeps and sulks.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, and the New Testament treats Jonah as a real person and his experience as a real sign. People debate whether the book is straight history or a pointed story, and whether a fish could swallow a man, but the point the writer presses is the same either way: you cannot flee the presence of God, and His mercy reaches even our enemies.
basic.originalLanguages: A few Hebrew words do a lot of work. God hurls the wind, the sailors hurl the cargo, and Jonah is hurled overboard, all the same word, tul, so God is clearly the one in control. The word great, gadol, keeps repeating: great city, great wind, great fish. And Jonah says he fears the LORD, yet it is the sailors who truly come to fear Him.
basic.typology: Jonah asleep in the storm, then thrown into the deep so that others may live, foreshadows Jesus asleep in a boat in a storm, and Jesus given up to death so that many are spared. Jesus Himself points back to Jonah as a sign.
basic.christ: Jesus calmed a storm with a word, showing He is the God of the sea that Jonah confessed. And where Jonah was thrown into the deep for his own sin, Jesus went willingly into death for the sins of others, the true and greater Jonah who saves those in the boat with Him.
basic.god: We learn that God is sovereign over the wind and the waves, that He pursues His runaway servants rather than abandoning them, and that His concern reaches far beyond Israel to pagan sailors and a wicked city. He is a God who will not let His people flee Him.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 139 says there is nowhere we can flee from God's presence. Mark 4 shows Jesus asleep in a storm and stilling it. Acts 10 shows the same God opening the door of mercy to the nations.
basic.oneStory: From the start Jonah shows that God's mercy was always meant to spill beyond Israel to the nations. A reluctant prophet, pagans coming to worship, a rescue through one man given up to the sea: it all points forward to the gospel going out to the whole world through Christ.

context.literary: The opening movement of a tightly crafted narrative, built on irony and repetition. Jonah keeps going down, to Joppa, into the ship, into sleep, and finally into the sea, while the pagan sailors keep moving up toward the true God. The scene is framed by the word of the LORD coming and by worship rising.
context.historical: Jonah son of Amittai was a real prophet from Gath-hepher in the northern kingdom, active under Jeroboam II in the eighth century BC, according to 2 Kings 14:25. Nineveh was a chief city of Assyria, the brutal empire that would later destroy the northern kingdom, which is why Jonah's flight is so charged.
context.canonical: One of the Twelve, unique among the prophetic books because it tells a story about a prophet rather than collecting his oracles. It sits within Scripture's larger concern for the nations, from the promise to Abraham that all peoples would be blessed, to Pentecost and the Gentile mission.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront Israel, and every reader, with a prophet who would rather die than see his enemies shown mercy, and so to expose the reader's own heart while displaying the reach of God's compassion and the futility of fleeing Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jonah's rebellion; it does not commend it. The abiding truths are that God is sovereign and inescapable, that He pursues His people in mercy even through discipline, and that His salvation is meant for the nations.

debates.question: Is the book of Jonah history or a didactic story?

views.label: Straightforward history
views.case: Jonah is a named historical prophet, 2 Kings 14:25, and Jesus speaks of the men of Nineveh who repented and of Jonah's three days as real events, Matthew 12:39-41. The most natural reading takes the account as reporting what happened.

views.label: Didactic narrative or satire
views.case: The book's heavy irony, symmetry, and satirical portrait of the prophet lead some to read it as a theological story making its point through art rather than reportage.

debates.bearing: The message stands either way, but the New Testament's appeal to Jonah as a sign fits most naturally with reading the events as real; the writer plainly intends to teach, whichever genre is granted.

debates.question: Could a great fish swallow a man and he live?

views.label: A genuine miracle
views.case: The text says the LORD appointed the fish; this is presented as a divine act, not a natural marvel, so the question is whether God can do it, and He can.

views.label: Symbol or hyperbole
views.case: Some take the fish as a symbolic or exaggerated image within a didactic tale.

debates.bearing: Read as history, the fish is a miracle of the God who made the sea; read as story, it still carries the truth of rescue from certain death. Jesus treats the three days as a real sign of His burial and resurrection.

study.originalLanguages: The load-bearing words move as a set. God hurls, tul, the wind; the sailors hurl the cargo; Jonah is hurled into the sea, so the same violent verb keeps God at the centre of the action. Everything is gadol, great, city and wind and storm and fish, stretching the scene to breaking point. Ra'ah, evil or calamity, links Nineveh's wickedness with the disaster on the sea. And yare, to fear or worship, exposes the irony: Jonah says he fears the LORD while fleeing Him, and it is the pagan sailors who end up fearing the LORD greatly.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus asleep in the boat, then stilling the storm with a word, Mark 4:38-39, revealing Himself as the God of the sea Jonah confessed.

typology.type: One man given to the sea so the others are spared
typology.fulfillment: Christ given up to death so that many are saved; the guilt-bearing rescue prefigures the cross, though Jonah suffers for his own sin and Jesus for ours.

typology.type: Pagan sailors brought to fear and worship the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The nations streaming to God, fulfilled as the gospel goes to the Gentiles, Acts 10 and 11.

study.christ: Jesus names Jonah as a sign of His own death and resurrection, Matthew 12:39-41, making this book part of how He explains Himself. Here in the first movement the links already appear: Jonah asleep in the storm anticipates Jesus asleep in the storm, yet Jesus rises and commands the wind and sea, proving He is the LORD who made the sea and the dry land, Jonah 1:9. Where Jonah is thrown into the deep for his own disobedience, Jesus goes willingly into the depths of death for the sins of others, the greater Jonah who calms the storm His people cannot survive.
study.god: The LORD is sovereign over creation, hurling the wind and stilling the sea at will. He is the God of heaven who made the sea and the dry land, so there is no place to flee His presence. He is relentless in mercy, pursuing His runaway prophet rather than discarding him. And His compassion is wide, already at work among pagan sailors, hinting that the mission to wicked Nineveh flows from a heart that loves the nations.

crossReferences.note: Jonah son of Amittai is a historical prophet under Jeroboam II, grounding the book in real history.
There is nowhere to flee from God's presence or Spirit, the very thing Jonah attempts.
Jesus asleep in the storm, then stilling it, echoing and surpassing Jonah.
God shows no partiality; He accepts those from every nation who fear Him, the trajectory Jonah resists.

study.oneStory: Jonah stands in the long story of God's mercy reaching past Israel to the nations, the promise to Abraham that all peoples would be blessed. A prophet flees that mercy, yet even in his flight pagans come to worship the true God. The rescue of many through one man cast into the sea points forward to Christ, and the sailors' worship anticipates the day the gospel would go to the ends of the earth.

sermon.title: Man Overboard!
sermon.why: Part of Begg's expository series A Study in Jonah, this message works through the storm and Jonah being cast into the sea, Jonah 1:4-17, showing God's sovereign pursuit of His fleeing prophet and His mercy toward the pagan sailors. A clear, orthodox treatment of this movement.

quiz.q: Why did Jonah flee toward Tarshish?

options.text: He did not want Nineveh, Israel's enemy, to receive God's mercy.
options.note: Jonah 4:2 tells us plainly: he fled because he knew God was gracious and would relent.

options.text: He was simply afraid of the dangerous journey to Nineveh.
options.note: Fear may have played a part, but the book names his real reason as resentment of God's mercy.

options.text: He had not actually heard God's call clearly.
options.note: The call was unmistakable; Jonah deliberately went the opposite way.

options.text: God had given him no reason to go at all.
options.note: God gave a clear reason: Nineveh's wickedness had come up before Him.

quiz.q: What does the repeated Hebrew verb tul, to hurl, show about the storm?

options.text: God is the true actor: He hurls the wind, and everything else is thrown about by it.
options.note: The same verb for the wind, the cargo, and Jonah keeps God at the centre of the scene.

options.text: The storm was a violent but ordinary weather event.
options.note: It was violent, but the text presents it as sent by God, not merely natural.

options.text: The sailors caused the storm by their poor seamanship.
options.note: The sailors are competent and desperate; the storm is God's doing.

options.text: The storm had nothing to do with Jonah.
options.note: The lots and Jonah's own words tie the storm directly to his flight.

quiz.q: What is striking about how the pagan sailors respond?

options.text: They move from fearing the storm to fearing and worshipping the LORD with sacrifice and vows.
options.note: The outsiders end up worshipping the true God while the prophet runs from Him.

options.text: They calm down once the cargo is thrown overboard.
options.note: They do lighten the ship, but the point is their turning to the LORD, not their seamanship.

options.text: They abandon their gods only out of superstition.
options.note: The text presents genuine reverence for the LORD, not mere superstition.

options.text: They remain hardened and never acknowledge God.
options.note: They fear the LORD greatly and offer sacrifice, the opposite of hardness.

quiz.q: How does Jonah in the storm point forward to Jesus?

options.text: Jonah asleep in the storm and given to the sea foreshadows Jesus asleep in a storm and given up to death to save others.
options.note: Jesus Himself makes Jonah a sign of His death and resurrection, Matthew 12:39-41.

options.text: Both simply happened to be caught in bad weather at sea.
options.note: There is a real echo, but it is more than coincidence; it is a deliberate pattern.

options.text: Jonah is a perfect model of obedience for Jesus to follow.
options.note: Jonah is disobedient here; Jesus is the greater and obedient Jonah.

options.text: There is no connection between Jonah and Jesus.
options.note: Jesus explicitly links Himself to the sign of Jonah.

quiz.q: What does Jonah's confession in verse 9 reveal?

options.text: He knows the LORD made the sea and the dry land, yet he is trying to flee that very God across the sea.
options.note: His theology is right while his life contradicts it, the book's central irony.

options.text: He is a faithful worshipper with nothing amiss in his walk.
options.note: His words are true, but his flight shows his heart is far from obedient.

options.text: He does not really believe in the LORD at all.
options.note: He confesses the LORD genuinely; his problem is rebellion, not unbelief.

options.text: He worships many gods like the sailors.
options.note: He confesses the one LORD, the God of heaven, over against their many gods.

questions: Jonah ran because he did not want his enemies to receive mercy. Is there anyone whose repentance and blessing you would secretly rather not see? What does that reveal about your own heart?
Jonah's theology was right, he knew who God was, but his life was running the other way. Where is there a gap between what you say you believe about God and how you are actually living?
The sailors, outsiders to the covenant, ended up worshipping God while the prophet slept. Where might God be working in people you had written off, and how does that challenge you?
Jonah discovered there is no fleeing the presence of God. Where are you trying to go down and away from what God has clearly called you to do?
One man was given to the sea so the others could live, and that points to Jesus given up for us. How does resting in His rescue free you to stop running and obey?

translationNotes.note: Tarshish was likely a distant port in the western Mediterranean, the opposite direction from Nineveh; the point is that Jonah flees as far as he can from the task and from God's presence.
I worship translates the Hebrew for I fear, the same root used of the sailors; Jonah claims to fear the LORD in the very act of fleeing Him.
The men rowed hard renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning the men dug in, picturing their desperate effort to reach shore rather than throw Jonah over.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jonah 1:1-16. Where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
